original_abstract: Neuroscience has historically served as a foundational catalyst for breakthroughs in artificial intelligence. We argue that further accelerating AI development requires a dedicated investment in NeuroAI—a research synthesis at the intersection of biological and artificial intelligence. Central to this mission is the "embodied Turing test," which challenges artificial agents to interact with the physical world with the same sensorimotor agility and fluidity as biological organisms. By shifting the benchmark from high-level cognitive tasks like chess or natural language processing toward the fundamental behaviors shared by all animals, we refocus the field on capabilities refined over 500 million years of evolution. Developing models capable of passing this embodied Turing test will provide a transformative roadmap for the next generation of artificial intelligence.
humanized_abstract: Over the years, Neuroscience has been a rapidly developing field of study which serves as a foundation for the progress of Artificial Intelligence. Thus, it becomes essential for the development of Artificial Intelligence to have a strong investment in NeuroAI which is a research synthesis of both biological and artificial intelligence. In this paper, we introduce a new benchmark of assessing an agent’s capability of acting similarly to biological organisms by passing the “embodied Turing test.” The embodied Turing test proposes that an embodied cognitive agent should be able to utilize its sensorimotor system to interact in the world in a similar fashion to that of animals. Hence, instead of assessing agents’ abilities through high-level cognitive tasks, such as playing chess or understanding languages, it evaluates the basic animal behaviors developed over 500 million years of evolution. In addition, it provides the cognitive models of agents with the potential for the future AI to act upon.

Hence, instead of assessing agents’ abilities through high-level cognitive tasks, such as playing chess or understanding languages, it evaluates the basic animal behaviors developed over 500 million years of evolution. In addition, it provides the cognitive models of agents with the potential for the future AI to act upon.
